planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring just as the shoots emerge; division every 3–5 years maintains vigor. Fortune's hosta is one of the most adaptable and vigorous of the species hostas and tolerates a wider range of light and soil conditions than many cultivars.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Fortune's hosta is one of the oldest and most influential species in hosta cultivation, first described in the 1880s by gardener and plant hunter Philip Franz von Siebold and named for plant hunter Robert Fortune. Modern research suggests it may be a naturally occurring hybrid of Japanese species, but it has been widely grown and hybridized for over 140 years. The medium-sized, heart-shaped, glossy dark green leaves are more tolerant of sun and heat than many larger-leaved hostas, and the plant is particularly vigorous and adaptable. Pale lavender flowers on tall scapes in midsummer attract hummingbirds. Dozens of named cultivars have been selected, including the popular 'Albomarginata' (cream-edged leaves) and 'Aureomarginata' (gold-edged leaves). More tolerant of Gulf Coast heat and humidity than many cultivated hostas.

planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; narrow-leaved hosta is among the most vigorous and adaptable of the species hostas and multiplies quickly into attractive clumps. More sun tolerant than most large-leaved hostas.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; narrow-leaved hosta is distinguished from most hostas by its notably long, lance-shaped (lanceolate) leaves — slender and pointed rather than the rounded, heart-shaped leaves typical of the genus. The glossy, dark green foliage forms attractive, arching, medium-sized mounds that remain fresh and attractive throughout the growing season. Deep purple, funnel-shaped flowers are produced in late summer on tall scapes — one of the latest-blooming species hostas, extending the season of hosta interest in the shade garden. This species has been cultivated in Japan for centuries and was one of the first hostas introduced to Western gardens. It is notably more tolerant of sun and summer heat than the large-leaved blue hostas, making it better suited to southern gardens. A reliable, vigorous species that forms substantial colonies over time in appropriate moist, shaded conditions.

planting.note: Plant in very well-drained, rocky, calcareous, alkaline to neutral crevices in partial shade to full sun; purple cliffbrake grows on dry limestone outcrops, rock faces, and cliff ledges and is exceptionally drought-tolerant for a fern. Do not plant in moist, shaded conditions.

directions: Plant in dry, rocky, calcareous, alkaline to neutral, well-drained crevices and ledges in partial shade to full sun; purple cliffbrake is one of the most drought-tolerant ferns in North America — an unusual xeric specialist that grows on dry limestone cliffs, ledges, and rocky outcrops where moisture is minimal and other ferns cannot survive. The wiry, dark purple-black stems (the trait giving the plant its name) emerge from rocky crevices with arching, blue-green, leathery fronds that curl in drought and rehydrate when rain returns. This extraordinary drought tolerance makes it the ideal fern for rock garden settings, dry stone walls, and limestone outcrops where moisture-loving ferns would decline. Entirely undemanding in dry, alkaline, rocky conditions; it declines in rich, moist, acidic soils where it is outcompeted by more vigorous plants. An outstanding rock garden fern with architectural, all-season interest.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; or scarify and cold stratify seeds for 60 days before spring sowing. Clustered poppy mallow has a deep taproot and transplants poorly — direct seeding in the final location is strongly preferred.

directions: Direct sow in lean, well-drained, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; clustered poppy mallow is a Midwestern prairie native related to the more commonly grown wine-cup (C. involucrata), producing the same vivid magenta-wine to deep purple-red, cup-shaped flowers but with a more upright, clustered growth habit rather than the sprawling form of wine-cup. The flowers are produced in clustered groups near the stem tips from early summer through fall. Like all Callirhoe, it develops a deep taproot that stores carbohydrates and enables the plant to survive drought and fire — making it extremely persistent in lean, dry, well-drained conditions once established. Decline occurs in rich, moist soils. An outstanding drought-tolerant native for dry prairie and meadow gardens where its brilliant color stands out against grasses and other drought-tolerant wildflowers.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; glade mallow grows naturally in the rich, moist limestone glades and streambanks of the Upper Midwest and Appalachians. It is dioecious — plant male and female plants for seed production.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; glade mallow is the only member of its genus — a tall, coarse-textured native perennial that is monotypic in the family Malvaceae, producing broadly palmately lobed leaves and small white flowers in summer. It grows naturally in the calcium-rich limestone glades and moist streambanks of the upper Midwest and eastern mountains, making it an unusual choice for alkaline, moist garden conditions. The plant is dioecious (separate male and female plants required for seed), an unusual trait in the mallow family. A rare plant in both the wild and in cultivation, glade mallow is a distinctive native specimen for large, naturalistic gardens with moist, alkaline, calcareous soils. Its bold, textured foliage and architectural height (to 6 feet) provide strong summer presence in the native garden.
